diagrams.title: Interrupt Processing Flow
diagrams.description: A sequential flowchart showing the complete lifecycle of interrupt handling from interrupt occurrence to return to normal execution
elements: Step 1: Interrupt source generates interrupt signal
Step 2: CPU completes current instruction execution
Step 3: Processor saves current state (PC, CPSR to LR)
Step 4: Processor switches to IRQ/FIQ mode
Step 5: Processor loads PC with interrupt vector address from Vector Table
Step 6: ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) executes
Step 7: ISR restores saved state from LR
Step 8: Return to interrupted program execution
Arrows showing sequential flow with branching for IRQ/FIQ paths
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the step-by-step sequence of interrupt processing, essential for understanding interrupt latency and designing ISRs

diagrams.title: ARM Interrupt Vector Table Structure
diagrams.description: Memory layout showing the ARM vector table with 8 exception vectors and their addresses
elements: Vector Table base address (0x00000000 or 0xFFFF0000 for high vectors)
8 vector entries in order: Reset, Undefined Instruction, Software Interrupt (SWI), Prefetch Abort, Data Abort, IRQ, FIQ
Each vector contains a branch instruction (LPC or MOV to PC)
Adjacent to vector table: IRQ handler address storage
Memory addresses shown (0x00, 0x04, 0x08, 0x0C, 0x10, 0x14, 0x18, 0x1C)
Arrows from each vector to corresponding handler routine
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the fixed structure of ARM vector table, understand vectored interrupts, and can locate handler addresses in memory for system design

diagrams.title: Interrupt Controller Architecture
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing how multiple external interrupt sources connect to CPU through an interrupt controller
elements: Multiple external devices (UART, Timer, GPIO, ADC, External Pin) as interrupt sources
Interrupt Controller (PLIC or VIC) block with priority encoder
IRQ and FIQ output lines to ARM processor
Interrupt Enable Register (IER) and Interrupt Pending Register (IPR) shown inside controller
Priority levels (0-7) assigned to each interrupt source
Software can enable/disable individual interrupts via enable register
Arrows showing signal flow from devices through controller to CPU
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how multiple interrupt sources are managed, the role of interrupt controllers in prioritization, and how to configure interrupts in microcontrollers

diagrams.title: Polling vs Interrupt-Driven I/O Comparison
diagrams.description: Two-panel diagram contrasting polling method with interrupt-driven method
elements: Left Panel - Polling: CPU continuously reads device status register in loop, CPU circles back repeatedly, CPU wastes cycles checking non-ready devices, device serviced only when CPU gets to it
Right Panel - Interrupt-Driven: CPU executes main program, device generates interrupt when ready, CPU immediately suspends and services device, CPU returns to main program
Time axis showing CPU utilization difference
Clear visual difference in efficiency between methods
diagrams.purpose: Students visually understand why interrupts are superior to polling, reinforcing the necessity of interrupts for efficient CPU utilization

diagrams.title: ARM Exception Modes and Stack Usage
diagrams.description: Diagram showing ARM processor modes and their associated banked registers relevant to interrupt handling
elements: 6 privilege modes shown: User (USR), System (SYS), Supervisor (SVC), IRQ, Abort (ABT), FIQ
Banked registers highlighted for IRQ and FIQ modes (r13_IRQ/r14_IRQ, SPSR_IRQ for IRQ; r8_FIQ-r14_FIQ, SPSR_FIQ for FIQ)
Stack pointers (SP) for each mode
Link Register (LR) showing where to return after exception
CPSR and SPSR registers shown
Mode transitions via arrows with exception types
Stack memory regions labeled for each mode
diagrams.purpose: Students understand ARM's banked register mechanism, how exceptions preserve state, and the importance of proper stack initialization for each mode

charts.title: Maskable vs Non-Maskable Interrupts
charts.content: Two-column comparison: Maskable Interrupts (IRQ) - Can be ignored by setting I bit in CPSR, Lower priority, Used for general peripherals (UART, Timer, GPIO), Can be enabled/disabled dynamically, More frequent in systems; Non-Maskable Interrupts (NMI/FIQ) - Cannot be ignored, Highest priority, Used for critical events (power failure, hardware fault), Always enabled, Reserved for most critical system events

charts.title: Interrupt Handling Decision Tree
charts.content: Flowchart showing how processor decides between interrupt sources: Power ON → Initialize Vector Table → Enable Interrors → Main Program Running → Interrupt Occurs? → YES → Check Priority → Check FIQ first (higher) → If FIQ: Save State → Execute FIQ Handler → Restore State → Return; If IRQ: Save State → Check IRQ Sources → Execute IRQ Handler → Restore State → Return; NO → Continue Main Program

charts.title: ARM Interrupt Source Classification
charts.content: Hierarchical classification: Interrupts divided into (1) Exceptions - Reset, Undefined Instruction, Software Interrupt (SWI), Prefetch Abort, Data Abort; (2) Interrupts - IRQ (General Purpose), FIQ (Fast Interrupt); Each type shows vector address, mode entered, and typical handler purpose

charts.title: Interrupt-Driven I/O vs DMA Transfer
charts.content: Comparison for data transfer of 1000 bytes: Interrupt-Driven I/O - CPU involved in each byte transfer, 1000 interrupts generated, High CPU overhead, Simple hardware (no DMA controller), Suitable for low data volumes; DMA Transfer - DMA controller handles data movement, Single interrupt for completion, Low CPU overhead, Complex hardware, Suitable for large data blocks (disk, network, audio)

branches: Definition - Signal requiring immediate attention, suspends current execution
Types - Hardware (external devices), Software/Traps (system calls), Exceptions (errors)
Key Components - Vector Table, ISR, Interrupt Controller, Priority Encoder
ARM Specific - IRQ (maskable), FIQ (fast/non-maskable), 7 exception modes
Performance - Latency (time to respond), Throughput (data rate)
Advanced - DMA reduces interrupts, Nested interrupts, Interrupt Coalescing
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all interrupt concepts, showing relationships between types, processing, hardware components, and performance considerations for exam preparation
